skill.name: mcp-resources-guide
skill.description: Guide developers in implementing MCP resources with URI design, listing, fetching, caching, streaming, and tests.
skill.summary: Build well-structured MCP resources for files, records, APIs, generated data, and large content.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: The skill is a Markdown guide with Rust examples, not an executable package. Most static findings are false positives from code fences and documentation text, but several examples show unsafe file, SQL, and API patterns that could influence generated code.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated File Resource Paths in Examples
medium_findings.description: The file resource examples parse URI paths and join them to a root path without canonicalization or traversal checks. If copied directly, these examples could expose files outside the intended resource root.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly derive filesystem paths from resource URIs and read them. The surrounding best practices mention path traversal, but the shown code does not enforce it.

medium_findings.title: SQL Identifier Constructed From Resource URI
medium_findings.description: The database resource example inserts the table name from the URI into a SQL string. Binding the record id helps, but the table identifier remains unvalidated and could lead to SQL injection if copied directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The table value is taken from parsed URI input and interpolated into the SQL query. This is a clear unsafe pattern for identifier handling.

medium_findings.title: API Example Sends Bearer Token to Constructed URL
medium_findings.description: The API resource example builds a URL from a base URL and URI-derived path, then sends an Authorization bearer token. It does not show host allowlisting, path normalization, or redirect handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is legitimate for API-backed resources, but the example omits validation controls. Risk depends on how a developer supplies base_url and handles redirects.

medium_findings.title: Symlink-Following Resource Listing Example
medium_findings.description: The documentation resource example follows symlinks while walking a docs directory. If copied without boundary checks, linked files outside the intended directory could be listed as resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly enables symlink following and strips the docs root prefix afterward. This can be safe only with additional canonical path checks not shown here.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown fenced code blocks and Rust examples. No shell execution instructions or executable scripts are present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are fenced Markdown examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file contains documentation content only.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Obfuscation Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and obfuscation alerts occur on front matter, headings, prose, and ordinary Rust snippets. I found no cryptographic API use and no prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no encryption, hashing, obfuscated payload, or instruction override. The alerts appear triggered by Markdown structure and common terms.

low_findings.title: Database and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The SQLite and reconnaissance alerts refer to generic database methods, URI parsing examples, and test names. They do not access local databases or inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are conceptual Rust examples for resources and tests. No filesystem database path, host probing, or reconnaissance behavior is present.

low_findings.title: API Key Alerts Are Not Environment Access
low_findings.description: The cited API key lines define a field and send an Authorization header in sample code. They do not read environment variables or collect secrets from the host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses a struct field named api_key, not environment access. The security concern is incomplete validation around API calls, covered separately as a medium finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path From URI Joined to Resource Root
dangerous_patterns.description: URI-derived paths are joined and read without canonical path validation. This can become path traversal in an implementation copied from the example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is parsed from the URI and used for file reads. No containment check appears in the example.

dangerous_patterns.title: SQL Query Uses URI-Derived Identifier
dangerous_patterns.description: The table name is interpolated into SQL from parsed URI content. Identifiers should be allowlisted instead of inserted from request input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible and only the record id is bound. This is a well-known SQL injection pattern for identifiers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent With Constructed Request
dangerous_patterns.description: The API example sends a bearer token with a URL assembled from configuration and URI path input. It should document allowlists and redirect policy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example is common and may be safe with trusted configuration. The risk is the missing validation guidance in a copyable example.

content.value_statement: MCP resource design can become inconsistent across files, APIs, databases, and generated data. This skill provides practical Rust patterns for URI design, listing, fetching, caching, streaming, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP resource concepts and differences from tools.
Provides URI pattern guidance for files, records, APIs, metrics, and templates.
Shows Rust examples for listing resources and fetching text or binary content.
Covers caching, lazy loading, MIME type handling, and large resource streaming.
Includes test scenarios for valid resources, invalid URIs, and expected MIME types.
Summarizes implementation roles from URI design through testing.
limitations: Examples are illustrative and may need security hardening before production use.
The guide focuses on Rust and the rmcp crate, not every MCP SDK.
It does not provide a complete runnable MCP server project.
It does not include detailed authorization or multi-tenant access control patterns.

use_cases.title: Design Resource Schemes
use_cases.description: Create consistent URI schemes for project files, database records, API data, and generated metrics.

use_cases.title: Implement Fetch and List Flows
use_cases.description: Translate resource requirements into Rust list and fetch handlers with metadata and MIME types.

use_cases.title: Review Resource Quality
use_cases.description: Check caching, streaming, validation, and tests before exposing context to AI assistants.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Resource Scheme
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new MCP resource type.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design MCP resource URI patterns for these data sources: [sources]. Include names, MIME types, and example URIs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a File Resource
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer adding file-backed resources in Rust.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a Rust rmcp resource implementation for files under [root]. Include listing, fetching, MIME detection, and path safety checks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Caching and Streaming
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP resource design and add caching, invalidation, and streaming behavior for large content: [design].

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Resource Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this MCP resource implementation for URI validation, path traversal, SQL injection, credential leakage, caching errors, and test gaps: [code].

output_examples.input: Design resources for project documentation and user records.
output_examples.output: A resource plan with separate docs and user URI schemes, metadata fields, MIME types, fetch behavior, and validation requirements.

output_examples.input: Improve a resource handler that reads files from a workspace.
output_examples.output: A review that identifies path normalization, root containment checks, MIME handling, binary support, and tests for invalid paths.

output_examples.input: Add caching to expensive generated reports.
output_examples.output: A caching strategy with TTL selection, cache keys based on resource URIs, invalidation triggers, and memory limits.

best_practices: Validate URI schemes, path segments, and query values before reading or querying data.
Use allowlists for database tables, API endpoints, MIME types, and exposed directories.
Add tests for invalid URIs, traversal attempts, missing resources, binary data, and large files.
anti_patterns: Do not join URI paths to filesystem roots without canonical containment checks.
Do not interpolate table names or other SQL identifiers from resource URIs.
Do not send credentials with requests to URLs that are not normalized and allowlisted.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build MCP resources that expose files, records, API data, generated content, and binary data to AI assistants.

faq.question: Which language does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Rust examples using the rmcp crate.

faq.question: Does it include a complete server?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that developers adapt inside their own MCP server.

faq.question: Can it help with resource performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers in-memory caching, lazy loading, and chunked reading for large resources.

faq.question: Is the sample code production ready?
faq.answer: No. Developers should add validation, authorization, allowlists, and path containment checks before production use.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
